label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is asking which of the statements (A to D) is true according to the passage. I need to go through each option and check against the information given in the text. First, let's recall the key points from the passage. The main topic is about the British government reviewing the use of lotteries to allocate school places because more than 20% of children are not getting into their first-choice schools in some areas. The passage mentions that in parts of the country, over 20% can't get into their first choice. For example, in Richmond-upon-Thames, 62% got their first choice, down from 64% the previous year, meaning 38% didn't. In Tower Hamlet, 71.1% got their first choice, so 28.9% didn't. The passage also says that more than 20% face disappointment in many areas. Now, looking at the options: Option A: "The lottery system is certainly a good way to allocate school places." The passage says the government is reviewing the system, but it doesn't explicitly state that it's "certainly a good way." The results in Brighton and Hove show an increase in first-choice placements, but also that over 5% didn't get any of their choices. So, the passage presents both pros and cons, so "certainly good" might be too strong. The government is reviewing it, which implies they are still evaluating, so A might not be correct. Option B: "Over one in five children fail to get into their first-choice schools in parts of Britain." The first sentence of the passage says "more than 20 percent of children are failing to get into their first-choice schools in parts of the country." Since 20% is one in five, this seems to directly match. The examples given in London and other areas back this up. So B looks correct. Option C: "The lottery system is welcomed by parents around twenty-five local authorities." The passage says that a small number of oversubscribed schools in twenty-five local authorities are using lotteries as per the government's suggestion. However, there's no mention of whether parents in these areas welcome it. The text doesn't state their reaction, so C is not supported.
